title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

texts.A: The grand and romantic dream of extensive high-speed rail networks spanning entire continents is fundamentally a nineteenth-century engineering solution being awkwardly applied to a genuinely complex twenty-first-century urban mobility challenge. In an era of widespread and normalized remote working and increasingly decentralized, polycentric urban development, the enormous and largely irreversible infrastructure cost of fixed-line rail is genuinely difficult to justify economically on current and projected usage patterns. [40]We should instead redirect our investments into intelligent fleets of autonomous electric shuttle vehicles capable of providing flexible, demand-responsive, point-to-point service wherever passengers actually need to travel.[/40] [39]The inherent adaptability and flexibility of the road network will always outperform the rigid, expensive, and geographically inflexible nature of fixed rail infrastructure.[/39] [37]Large-scale public infrastructure projects of this kind are increasingly becoming costly political white elephants that serve the personal ambitions of elected officials rather than the genuine daily mobility needs of the modern, dispersed commuter population.[/37]
texts.B: Individualized private transport, even in its most technologically advanced electric and autonomous configuration, will simply never resolve the fundamental and mathematically intractable problem of urban geometry: the finite and extremely precious commodity of physical street space in our increasingly crowded and densely populated cities. [38]A hundred autonomous passenger pods, however efficiently routed and intelligently managed, inevitably occupy dramatically more road and parking space than a single high-capacity commuter train moving the same total number of passengers.[/38] I firmly agree that we must urgently modernize and decarbonize our urban transport infrastructure, but passenger rail remains the only genuinely and structurally proven method of moving large numbers of people efficiently, rapidly, and cleanly between dense urban centers. [40]The widely celebrated 'flexibility' of the private car is precisely what produced the sprawling, disconnected, and resource-intensive suburban development patterns now widely recognized as a primary driver of both chronic social isolation and accelerating environmental degradation.[/40]
texts.C: The most consequential revolution in transport policy isn't about the specific type of vehicle we deploy, but about the underlying 'platform' and business model that organizes and delivers mobility as a genuinely accessible service. We are transitioning toward a 'Mobility as a Service' paradigm where the traditional rigid distinction between public and private transport gradually and meaningfully dissolves from the perspective of the end user. [39]I share the view that expensive fixed-line rail infrastructure is too slow to construct and too inflexible to adapt adequately to rapidly changing demographic patterns and evolving urban development priorities.[/39] However, the answer is most certainly not more private cars of any technological configuration. The solution lies in a sophisticated, data-driven hybrid system of 'micro-mobility'—electric bikes, cargo cycles, and scooters—seamlessly connected to a reliable backbone of high-frequency rapid bus transit on dedicated corridors. Buses remain dramatically cheaper to deploy, faster to adapt, and more socially equitable than passenger rail.
texts.D: The frequently repeated economic argument against investing in high-speed rail infrastructure conveniently ignores the enormous and well-documented carbon footprint of short-haul aviation, which passenger rail is demonstrably and perfectly positioned to replace on routes under three hours in duration. [37]While the upfront capital costs of rail infrastructure are admittedly substantial, the long-term economic, social, and environmental dividends for the regions and communities they serve are genuinely undeniable and comprehensively documented by independent research.[/37] [38]I fundamentally disagree with the proposition that 'point-to-point' autonomous pods represent a viable or desirable mobility alternative at any meaningful urban scale; they would inevitably produce greater congestion, accelerate suburban sprawl, and further erode the important shared social dimension of collective public travel.[/38] We urgently need a transport infrastructure that actively and physically brings diverse communities together rather than one that permanently seals individuals inside isolated private technological bubbles and quietly but effectively reinforces social fragmentation and atomization.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the long-term value of high-speed rail projects?
questions.explanation: Expert A calls rail a '19th-century solution' and a 'white elephant.' Expert D argues that its 'long-term economic and environmental dividends are undeniable.'

questions.question: Which expert shares B's concern about the space efficiency of individualized transport?
questions.explanation: Expert B notes that pods take up more 'road space' than a train. Expert D agrees, stating that pods would 'lead to even more congestion' and decline in shared experience.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to C regarding the drawbacks of fixed-line rail systems?
questions.explanation: Expert C says rail is 'too slow to adapt' to changing demographics. Expert A also mentions the 'rigidity of the track' as a major downside compared to road flexibility.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the social impact of car-centric infrastructure?
questions.explanation: Expert B argues car-centricity leads to 'social isolation' and 'environmental decay.' Expert A views the 'flexibility of the road' as a positive that serves the 'needs of the modern commuter' better than rail.
